channel.title: Articles on I Chingon
channel.description: Recent content in Articles on I Chingon

item.title: What do the I Ching and the Japanese creation myth have in common?
item.description: The connection between Hexagram 1 of the I Ching and the creation myth of the Kojiki is structural and linguistic. The creators of the Japanese myth did not copy the story, but they did adopt the philosophical language and cosmogonic model of the I Ching to narrate their own origins.

item.title: The I Ching and the Universe that Computes Itself
item.description: From antiquity to particle colliders and artificial intelligence, the most fundamental question persists: What is the universe? In this article, we propose a bold synthesis: the universe is not made of solid matter, but of information. The I Ching, with its binary system of Yin and Yang, anticipated this idea long ago. Today, quantum physics shows us that reality, at its most fundamental level, behaves like ‘running software’. But if the universe is code, where does consciousness fit in? Are we mere spectators, or are we the universe processing itself? We explore this paradox through the lens of Eastern philosophy (Tao Te Ching, Buddhism) and the challenges posed by modern science, such as David Chalmers’ ‘Hard Problem of Consciousness’.

item.title: The Symbolism of Holy Week Through the Lens of the I Ching
item.description: We explore how the I Ching encodes the mystery of Christ’s death and resurrection in hexagrams 23 (Disintegration), 2 (The Earth), and 24 (The Return), revealing Holy Saturday as a timeless state and the heart of both processes.

item.title: Is the I Ching the First Meme in History? 🤯
item.description: The I Ching is not just an ancient oracle, but the ancestor of all modern memes. This article explores how the 64 hexagrams align with Richard Dawkins’ theory and behave just like the ‘distracted boyfriend’ or any internet viral. An original take bridging ancient philosophy and 21st‑century digital culture.

item.title: How to Consult the I Ching Using the Coin Toss Method
item.description: A practical guide to consulting the I Ching by tossing three coins. It explains the numerical value of each throw, how the 6 hexagram lines are built, the difference between fixed and moving lines, and how to identify the resulting and derived hexagrams.

item.title: The ABC of the I Ching
item.description: The I Ching is more than an oracle: it’s a universal language based on Yin and Yang. This article guides you through its binary alphabet, the 8 trigrams, and the 64 hexagrams, explaining how its words and sentences are structured.

item.title: Chance, Science, and the I Ching: A Convergence in Understanding Order and Uncertainty
item.description: Is chance a tool for knowledge? This article connects the I Ching with quantum mechanics, chaos theory, Gödel’s theorems and bootstrapping to show how chance, far from being an obstacle, rather filters our subjectivity and allows us to access deeper orders.
